blockers.raw: C3 backfill urgency_source semantics: when backfill uses the identical deterministic classifier as write-time, should backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword' or 'tp_distance') with a separate backfill flag, or stamp a blanket 'backfill_heuristic' to distinguish post-hoc classification from write-time? Currently C3 §3 lists 'backfill_heuristic' as an allowed enum value, but §5.5.3 mandates the identical classifier is used for both paths. These two statements are in tension for audit/drift semantics downstream (C4/C6 breach history, KPI segmentation).

extra.question: Cutover requires Mike's explicit go-ahead
signal-gateway.service systemd re-enable or keep manual?
.openclaw/workspace fork-sync or re-point to canonical?

extra.answer: Re-enable the systemd unit before B4 cutover. Reasons: (1) Arbiter PHASE-0 §Resilience explicitly models signal-gateway supervision as systemd watchdog in the 'what OinkFarm already does well' column — manual nohup regressed that strength; (2) operational hygiene for cutover — re-enabling systemd is the interim step toward docker-compose and avoids creating an orphan process for B13 to untangle; (3) restart procedure clarity during the CRITICAL B4 window — `systemctl restart signal-gateway` is one command vs a failure-prone pkill/nohup/redirect incantation. Risk: low — if disabled for a reason, reverting takes 5 minutes but the reason should be documented and fixed now. OinkV to diff .DISABLED unit vs live nohup command to confirm env-vars match.

Fork-sync for B4; schedule re-point for B13 (Docker Compose). Rationale: (1) B4 is a CRITICAL cutover window — re-pointing service WorkingDirectories mid-cutover is out of scope and risky; fork-sync (single cp + commit) is atomic and reversible; (2) B13 re-writes deployment topology anyway — when services move into Docker containers the WorkingDirectory/volume mount is rebuilt against canonical paths, so re-pointing now creates work B13 undoes; (3) BS-16 semantic-drift risk is mitigated by the Q-B1-2 policy — canonical + CI-enforced byte-identical vendored copies. FORGE to add re-point as a B13 deliverable; OinkV to run continuous byte-equality check until B13 ships.

extra.answer: Same server (barn) initially, inside Docker Compose network. Matches Arbiter V3 PHASE-4 §2 topology exactly ('Container definitions for 8 services + PostgreSQL + Redis'). Trigger for re-evaluation: if measured PostgreSQL contention on barn exceeds 50% CPU sustained or I/O wait >20%, move PG to a dedicated VPS. Until then, co-location is correct. Risk: low — PG is easy to move with pg_dump/pg_restore; the B1 abstraction layer isolates services from physical location (only OINK_PG_URL changes).

extra.text: FORGE fresh-eyes cross-check complete. Agree with VIGIL on A11, B1, and B5. For B2, I agree the reviewed PR artifact (oinkfarm#153 / commit 17073d12, plus vendor PRs) earned a PASS, but I do not agree that this review safely covers the current deployment candidate. Live oinkfarm working tree at be2ff3b7 has uncommitted post-review migration changes: discord_message_id/channel_id widened to VARCHAR(64), fill_status widened to VARCHAR(30), and leverage text coercion added to migrate_sqlite_to_pg.py. Rollback audit: transactional pre-commit rollback is solid, but rerun safety is operational rather than idempotent; post-commit reruns require fresh DB/dropdb semantics. Runtime check: OINK_DB_DUAL_WRITE is still off everywhere, system Python has psycopg 3.3.3, live signal-gateway uses system Python with OINK_PG_URL set, but repo-local signal-gateway venv still lacks psycopg, leaving a reproducibility/containerization gap. extra.commit_subject: Merge anvil/B1-db-abstraction — Task B1: database abstraction layer (oink_db.py)

extra.question: PostgreSQL driver: psycopg3 vs psycopg2?
oink_db.py location?

extra.answer: Adopt psycopg3 (`psycopg[binary]`). It is the actively maintained upstream (psycopg2 is in maintenance mode), has a cleaner Connection/Cursor interface matching FORGE's B1 abstraction-layer design, supports the %s parameter style the B1 regex translator targets, and provides free async optionality. psycopg2 offers nothing psycopg3 lacks for OinkFarm. Risk: very low — drop-in replacement at the abstraction layer if incompatibilities arise.

Canonical oink_db.py lives in oinkfarm scripts/ (/home/oinkv/oinkfarm/scripts/oink_db.py), with vendored copies in oink-sync/ and signal-gateway/ tracked by an automated fork-sync CI step. Cross-repo imports are physically impossible (separate repos), so pure single-canonical is not feasible; vendoring pattern is already used for event_store.py. B4 plan §4a.-1 specifies the fork-sync procedure; this codifies it permanently for each Phase B merge. Semantic-drift risk (BS-16) is managed by forbidding edits to vendored copies — changes go to canonical, then a sync commit propagates. OinkV to audit byte-identical pre-merge.

extra.answer: expand_guard_block_phase1_until_proof
extra.rationale: B9 is W1 immutable signal records — the whole point is immutability. A guard that only covers OinkConnection.execute() but misses cursor.execute, executemany, or the wrapper classes creates bypass paths that silently defeat W1. Block Phase 1 until Anvil greps all write surfaces in oinkfarm + signal-gateway + oink-sync and proves coverage. This is exactly the kind of hole #189 represents at a different layer — we don't accept partial guards on immutability promises.

extra.question: Redis hosting: same server or separate?
Redis Streams retention policy: MAXLEN or time-based?

extra.answer: Same server (barn) as Docker container, 127.0.0.1 binding, internal-only port exposure. Matches Arbiter V3 PHASE-4 §2 single-topology Docker Compose model ('8 services + PostgreSQL + Redis'). 256MB RAM allocation for Redis is trivial on barn. Redis MAXLEN retention (Q-HH-2) caps memory footprint. Trigger for re-evaluation: if Redis CPU >30% sustained or stream lag shows contention with PG writes. Risk: low — Redis is even easier to relocate than PG (stateless streams with AOF persistence; redis-cli --rdb dump).
Approximate MAXLEN retention per-topic. ingestion.raw ~10000, notification.outbound ~5000, lifecycle.event ~10000. Tunable at runtime.

extra.answer: constrain_shadow_to_2_topics_until_b15
extra.rationale: Shadow-mode is for validating one thing at a time. The ratified B12 plan is 2 topics (ingestion.raw + notification.outbound), not 8. Expanding to 8 topics before B15 schema versioning is spec'd creates 6 topics with undefined envelope contracts, which is precisely the schema-drift problem B15 exists to prevent. Keep shadow at 2 topics. Other 6 topics become observed in sequence after B15 lands. This is tiny cost (~2 days of staging work) vs preventing 6 topics of schema-migration pain.

extra.answer: require_contract_valid_for_lifecycle_and_validation
extra.rationale: lifecycle.event and validation.result are exactly the topics B11 (event-walk) and future consumers will build correctness guarantees on. Fail-closed at the publish boundary for these two topics. Schema-invalid envelope = drop + log, not publish. The publish-then-observe failure counter pattern is fine for low-criticality/non-authoritative topics but not for the topics that feed into our data-purity substrate.

extra.question: Track 2 event-walk close_source flip from dual-compute to authoritative: fixed date vs SLO (<=0.1% divergence for 7 consecutive days)?
Where to store MICRO_GATE_DECISION rows for pre-INSERT rejections (no signal_id yet)?

extra.answer: slo_gated_7day
extra.rationale: Data-purity endgame requires objective evidence of divergence convergence, not calendar-based wishful thinking. SLO <=0.1% divergence for 7 consecutive days gives the same kind of empirical gate as B3's 7-day reconciliation window — Mike's own Q-B3-2 ruling establishes 7d+reset as the house rule for cutover gates. Fixed-date flip risks authoritativeness before numbers agree. If divergence persists, event-walk stays dual-compute mode indefinitely — that's the correct fail-open behavior for a data-purity system.

extra.answer: signal_events_with_null_signal_id
extra.rationale: Single source of truth is the whole point of B11 event substrate. Separate table fragments queries — every PnL walker, every anomaly detector, every audit now needs UNION across two tables. Pre-INSERT rejections are rare (micro-gate B14-style), so null FK is not a hot path. Add a surrogate key `pre_insert_ref` (hash of ticker+trader+timestamp+attempted_sl) to the MICRO_GATE_DECISION extra field so if the rejected signal LATER makes it through (e.g., after reconciliation retry), we can retroactively link. Single-table, null-FK beats two-table split for data-purity readability.

extra.question: Confidence routing: hard reject or soft flag?
Route low-confidence signals to PROVISIONAL state or hard-reject?

extra.answer: Soft flag via PROVISIONAL lifecycle state. Zero data loss. Confidence threshold is a C2 tuning parameter, not hard-coded.

extra.question: For signals below the configured confidence threshold, should the system hard_reject them from the live lifecycle, or soft_flag_provisional them into a review queue?

extra.answer: SOFT-FLAG. Below-threshold confidence signals pass through the pipeline with a visual marker ('low-confidence' label) on the dashboard, NOT dropped. Rationale: (1) 'We see all the data, clearly labeled' philosophy — consistent with data-purity endgame; (2) reversibility — hard-reject loses historical context, we can't retroactively analyze signals we dropped; (3) user-side filterability — any operator (Mike, Dominik) can filter on the dashboard based on the label; (4) C5 anomaly detection depends on having full signal history including low-confidence ones to detect drift patterns. Implementation note for FORGE: the C2 plan should specify the label taxonomy (e.g., 'low-conf', 'medium-conf', 'high-conf' with explicit percentile thresholds) and ensure the DB schema carries the confidence score through to every downstream query, not just a boolean flag.
soft_flag_provisional
extra.note: Below-threshold signals pass through as PROVISIONAL with dashboard/API visibility; no hard reject path.
Q-HH-5 locked to soft_flag_provisional; verified surfaces updated to /signals + dashboard files.
Soft-flag policy remains locked; 2026-04-22 Mike threshold directive flagged as Q-C2-2 conflict (display bands vs routing)

extra.question: Mike 2026-04-22 00:12 threshold directive (<50/50-75/>=75) conflicts with Q-HH-5 ratified bootstrap (<0.75/0.75-0.89/>=0.90) and empirical min confidence 0.60 (new <0.50 band empirically empty). Which interpretation?

extra.answer: render_only_bands_change_routing_stays_0.75
extra.rationale: Routing threshold 0.75 stays — it's empirically calibrated to actual signal-quality distribution and Q-HH-5 SOFT-FLAG decision applies below 0.75. Dashboard labels shift to <0.50 / 0.50-0.75 / >=0.75 for human readability (matches my earlier framing). Empirically the <0.50 band will be empty day 1 since min observed confidence is 0.60 — that's FINE. Empty band today = capacity to flag quality degradation tomorrow (new trader with low conviction, upstream parser drift, etc.). Zero data loss, just labeling clarity. Do NOT drop routing to 0.50 — queue would be empty and the whole routing machinery has no work.

extra.question: C3 backfill urgency_source semantics: when backfill uses the identical deterministic classifier as write-time, should backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword' or 'tp_distance') with a separate backfill flag, or stamp a blanket 'backfill_heuristic' to distinguish post-hoc classification from write-time? Currently C3 §3 lists 'backfill_heuristic' as an allowed enum value, but §5.5.3 mandates the identical classifier is used for both paths. These two statements are in tension for audit/drift semantics downstream (C4/C6 breach history, KPI segmentation).
options: A: keep 'backfill_heuristic' as the stamped urgency_source for all backfilled rows, dropping natural-source fidelity for those rows (simple audit flag, but loses evidence chain)

B: backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword'), and 'backfill_heuristic' is removed from the enum; a separate immutable 'classified_at_backfill' boolean column distinguishes post-hoc rows (preserves evidence chain, adds one column)

C: backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source AND backfill adds a second companion column 'urgency_classified_phase' ∈ {ingest, backfill} — keeps enum pure, makes provenance explicit at the row level
extra.forge_recommendation: Option C — evidence fidelity (what fired) and timing provenance (when it fired) are orthogonal and should live in separate columns. Option A collapses them and loses audit value; Option B works but a boolean is less extensible if a future re-backfill round is needed.

extra.text: FORGE agrees with VIGIL REVISE 4.9 on M197 — no disagreement, no DECISION_NEEDED. Adding Phase C purity implication VIGIL did not escalate: close_source upgrades without signal_events emission silently diverge the B11 event-walk from the legacy column, which is exactly what the C4 divergence feed (TASK-C4 §5.3) and PHASE-C-DATA-PURITY-INVARIANTS.md event-walk invariant consume. Upgrade-without-event = invisible drift. R2 must (a) emit CLOSE_SOURCE_UPGRADED signal_event {old_source,new_source,old_tier,new_tier,reason,raw_text_snippet} in the SAME transaction as the UPDATE (drop the independent conn.commit()); (b) ensure B11 v2 event-walk consumes this event so post-B11 close_source derivation stays consistent with the legacy column; (c) add tests: event emission assertion, single-transaction atomicity, C4 divergence probe returns zero mismatch after upgrade.

extra.answer: Adopt psycopg3 (`psycopg[binary]`). It is the actively maintained upstream (psycopg2 is in maintenance mode), has a cleaner Connection/Cursor interface matching FORGE's B1 abstraction-layer design, supports the %s parameter style the B1 regex translator targets, and provides free async optionality. psycopg2 offers nothing psycopg3 lacks for OinkFarm. Risk: very low — drop-in replacement at the abstraction layer if incompatibilities arise.

Canonical oink_db.py lives in oinkfarm scripts/ (/home/oinkv/oinkfarm/scripts/oink_db.py), with vendored copies in oink-sync/ and signal-gateway/ tracked by an automated fork-sync CI step. Cross-repo imports are physically impossible (separate repos), so pure single-canonical is not feasible; vendoring pattern is already used for event_store.py. B4 plan §4a.-1 specifies the fork-sync procedure; this codifies it permanently for each Phase B merge. Semantic-drift risk (BS-16) is managed by forbidding edits to vendored copies — changes go to canonical, then a sync commit propagates. OinkV to audit byte-identical pre-merge.

Same server (barn) initially, inside Docker Compose network. Matches Arbiter V3 PHASE-4 §2 topology exactly ('Container definitions for 8 services + PostgreSQL + Redis'). Trigger for re-evaluation: if measured PostgreSQL contention on barn exceeds 50% CPU sustained or I/O wait >20%, move PG to a dedicated VPS. Until then, co-location is correct. Risk: low — PG is easy to move with pg_dump/pg_restore; the B1 abstraction layer isolates services from physical location (only OINK_PG_URL changes).

Re-enable the systemd unit before B4 cutover. Reasons: (1) Arbiter PHASE-0 §Resilience explicitly models signal-gateway supervision as systemd watchdog in the 'what OinkFarm already does well' column — manual nohup regressed that strength; (2) operational hygiene for cutover — re-enabling systemd is the interim step toward docker-compose and avoids creating an orphan process for B13 to untangle; (3) restart procedure clarity during the CRITICAL B4 window — `systemctl restart signal-gateway` is one command vs a failure-prone pkill/nohup/redirect incantation. Risk: low — if disabled for a reason, reverting takes 5 minutes but the reason should be documented and fixed now. OinkV to diff .DISABLED unit vs live nohup command to confirm env-vars match.

Fork-sync for B4; schedule re-point for B13 (Docker Compose). Rationale: (1) B4 is a CRITICAL cutover window — re-pointing service WorkingDirectories mid-cutover is out of scope and risky; fork-sync (single cp + commit) is atomic and reversible; (2) B13 re-writes deployment topology anyway — when services move into Docker containers the WorkingDirectory/volume mount is rebuilt against canonical paths, so re-pointing now creates work B13 undoes; (3) BS-16 semantic-drift risk is mitigated by the Q-B1-2 policy — canonical + CI-enforced byte-identical vendored copies. FORGE to add re-point as a B13 deliverable; OinkV to run continuous byte-equality check until B13 ships.

Same server (barn) as Docker container, 127.0.0.1 binding, internal-only port exposure. Matches Arbiter V3 PHASE-4 §2 single-topology Docker Compose model ('8 services + PostgreSQL + Redis'). 256MB RAM allocation for Redis is trivial on barn. Redis MAXLEN retention (Q-HH-2) caps memory footprint. Trigger for re-evaluation: if Redis CPU >30% sustained or stream lag shows contention with PG writes. Risk: low — Redis is even easier to relocate than PG (stateless streams with AOF persistence; redis-cli --rdb dump).

extra.text:
FORGE opened a new parallel track this afternoon: Task 171 ("OinXtractor as a stateful retrieval-learning agent") — the plan is to give the trader-message extractor actual memory, so it recalls the last few extractions from the same trader as few-shot guidance, captures every successful extraction back into Supermemory, and turns OinkV's correction flag into a learning loop instead of a one-off fix. FORGE replaced the first draft with a tighter "Direct Execution Sequence" an hour later — five commits, ~2.5 hours of work total, no Mike gates (you already granted full autonomous authority on this one), runs parallel to Heavy Hybrid Phase B rather than waiting in line behind it.

GUARDIAN's pre-change accuracy baseline for 171 landed at 18:02 CEST and it's honest in a useful way: we don't actually have a real per-trader weekly extraction-accuracy KPI in production today. The baseline is a proxy pulled from OinkV's audit journal for the last 7 days — posture is clean (zero confirmed bad DB writes, zero confirmed missed signals, two safe-noise incidents that got rejected before doing damage), but it's flagged PROXY_ONLY because the underlying measurement gap is part of what 171 is meant to close. So the "did the learning agent actually help" question only becomes answerable once 171 ships.

Phase B side is quiet — no new merges, PRs, or canary verdicts in the last two hours. ANVIL has been silent since lunch, which is normal for a post-Wave-2 review lull. B3's 7-day reconciliation clock toward the 2026-04-26 B4 cutover is still ticking clean, and the B2 canary plus B6/B7/B8 post-deploy monitoring reports are the next expected signals. Nothing on the board needs you today.

Spot-audit on Vigil: there are still no newly persisted review artifacts from the post-restart drain, so there is nothing real to score as an opus-4-7 upgrade yet. The latest completed review files remain the strong opus-4-6 baseline (for example B7 scored 10.00 and B5 scored 9.85 with evidence-dense reasoning and clean rollback/data-integrity checks). In-flight work on PRs #9 and #11 looks baseline-consistent, not worse: Vigil is verifying PR heads, separating pre-existing environment failures from change-caused failures, and not hand-waving evidence. One real anomaly: session telemetry is inconsistent right now, because the Vigil status card still reports claude-opus-4-6 while the current transcript contains fresh gpt-5.4 tool-trace turns. So my finding is: no review-quality regression observed yet, but the model-rotation state itself is not clean enough to claim an opus-4-7 improvement.

OinXtractor monitoring check: routine artifact republishes are healthy and the latest quality artifacts refreshed at 12:02 local. The latency proxy is not alarming, with 24h p50 around 177.5s and same-day hourly samples down around 86s by 09:00Z. But there is a serious signal in the metrics payload that should not be ignored: unknown_rate shows 100.0% for both 24h (45/45) and 7d (230/230) even though the artifact still labels unknown_rate_status=ok and says the target is <5%. That is either a monitoring bug or a real quality regression, but in either case it is not a benign republish. I am flagging it as a quality-warning condition until the metric definition is reconciled. Separately, recent upstream extractor defects on sub-$1 assets and malformed HYPE payloads remain open evidence that extraction quality is not fully green.

A171 current state: the original hard blocker was partially cleared only because a mitigation path was found, not because the design landed. The runtime still has no working per-agent session-history cap for OinXtractor. Per-agent contextPruning was rejected at runtime, and the confirmed supported knobs are only global contextPruning plus per-agent contextLimits for memory/tool budgets. Active mitigation remains: extractor tool allowlist trimmed, lossless-claw freshTailCount reduced to 8, and supermemory autoRecall enabled for oinxtractor_patterns. That combination reduced overflow pressure enough to get post-patch turns through, but it does not satisfy the original A171 design goal. So the correct sprint read is: mitigated, not complete, and still carrying a real design gap until per-agent history control or an explicit alternate design is ratified.

extra.text: FORGE fresh-eyes cross-check complete. Agree with VIGIL on A11, B1, and B5. Live oinkfarm working tree at be2ff3b7 has uncommitted post-review migration changes: discord_message_id/channel_id widened to VARCHAR(64), fill_status widened to VARCHAR(30), and leverage text coercion added to migrate_sqlite_to_pg.py. extra.text:
Guardian just ran back-to-back phase-0 reviews and the headline is a good catch on B9 (W1 immutable signal records — the INSERT-only enforcement that's the whole point of the data-truth rebuild). Guardian rejected the proposal with REQUEST_CHANGES at the critical tier because the W1 write-guard, as drafted, would have blanket-blocked UPDATEs on the signals table — which would have broken live price syncing, since engine.py legitimately updates ephemeral pricing columns. Guardian also found ten additional update sites beyond what Anvil's proposal covered, and flagged that an automated reconciliation gate needs to be a precondition for moving from phase 1 to phase 2. Anvil goes into a revision round; nothing about this is alarming — it's exactly the kind of thing the two-reviewer loop exists to catch before code gets written.

On the same sweep Guardian approved B12 (Redis Streams — the message bus between the decomposed services) with four advisory notes, one of which is worth knowing: Redis here is transport only, not storage, so PostgreSQL stays authoritative. Separately, Forge escalated a new question for Mike on C2 (signal confidence handling) — Q-HH-5: for signals that land below the configured confidence threshold, does the system hard-reject them from the live lifecycle, or does it soft-flag them as provisional into a review queue? Forge recommends soft-flag-provisional. This is a genuine product decision, not a Hermes-ops call, so it's parked for Mike.

Housekeeping hasn't moved since the last update: the same five PRs (A11 #133, B1 #149 and #21, B2 #24, B5 #25) are still carrying the 24h-no-review flag, and Vigil hasn't come back for round two. Anvil and Forge are both still warm. A10's 48h canary window closes at 18:29 UTC and remains the next scheduled event.

extra.answer: SOFT-FLAG. Below-threshold confidence signals pass through the pipeline with a visual marker ('low-confidence' label) on the dashboard, NOT dropped. Rationale: (1) 'We see all the data, clearly labeled' philosophy — consistent with data-purity endgame; (2) reversibility — hard-reject loses historical context, we can't retroactively analyze signals we dropped; (3) user-side filterability — any operator (Mike, Dominik) can filter on the dashboard based on the label; (4) C5 anomaly detection depends on having full signal history including low-confidence ones to detect drift patterns. Implementation note for FORGE: the C2 plan should specify the label taxonomy (e.g., 'low-conf', 'medium-conf', 'high-conf' with explicit percentile thresholds) and ensure the DB schema carries the confidence score through to every downstream query, not just a boolean flag.

Quiet 90 minutes on the merge line, but ANVIL just closed out the full Mike-checklist at once — five proposals are now stacked in the review queue. B4 R2 (the PostgreSQL cutover) is back in, B9 R2 (W1 immutable signal records — the INSERT-only guard that anchors the data-truth rebuild) is re-submitted after Guardian's R1 request-for-changes, plus fresh Phase 0 drafts for B10 (W3 materialized views, with a compute_blended_pnl() SQL function to verify PnL continuity through the cutover) and B11 (adjacent Wave 3 scope), and B12 (Redis Streams transport) R1 is still open. Guardian came back on B9 and APPROVED the R2 revision — both critical-tier R1 blockers are resolved: the write-guard now uses a 3-tier column classification so engine.py's live-price UPDATEs don't get blanket-blocked, and Anvil added a reconcile_w1.py gate that requires 14 consecutive reconciliation PASSes plus at least 10 sample signals before Phase 2 can cut over. B9 is now waiting on VIGIL's R2 alongside the other four. Separately, Forge resolved Q-HH-5 on C2 (signal confidence handling) without parking it on Mike — below-threshold signals will pass through as PROVISIONAL with dashboard and API visibility, no hard-reject path, per Forge's original recommendation. The big story this half-hour is ISSUE-189, a production data-integrity bug GUARDIAN surfaced during the sprint poke: some stop-loss-moved-to-break-even trades are getting rejected by micro-gate B14 and logged as full losses when they actually closed flat. GUARDIAN's full backfill scan landed 143 candidate trades affected — 10.7x the preliminary estimate we had going in, which is a meaningfully bigger blast radius than first thought. ANVIL drafted the Phase 0 proposal to scope the fix, GUARDIAN approved it about four minutes later (the CRITICAL 4-hour SLA was a non-event), so the pipeline is already moving on TASK-189 without needing a Mike intervention.

On the planned work: FORGE shipped the B11 plan (the remaining emit-stream piece) and the plan surfaced two fresh questions that need Mike when he's back. Q-B11-4 asks whether we flip Track 2's event-walk close_source from dual-compute to authoritative on a fixed calendar date or only after an SLO clears (≤0.1% divergence for 7 consecutive days) — i.e. do we commit to a deadline or let the data decide. Q-B11-5 asks where to store the MICRO_GATE_DECISION rows for pre-INSERT rejections that don't yet have a signal_id — a small schema-shape call but it needs to be made before ANVIL codes it.

Background is fine: B2's canary is still open, all previously-merged tasks stay covered, OinkV's spot-audits keep coming back clean. The only real asks on the human side are the two new B11 gates above — and those can wait until tomorrow without blocking anything.

🪽 Scribe · 00:19 UTC

Heads up — the scribe cron hit a bug and couldn't produce tonight's narrative. The script blew up on line 50 of sprint-scribe.py with a `KeyError: 'event_id'`, which means at least one event in the stream is missing the `event_id` field that the filter expects. Since it crashed before reading the delta, I don't have a sprint update for you this round — no data was lost, just skipped.

Likely cause: an emitter somewhere wrote an event without the `event_id` key (schema drift or a partial write), and the filter assumes every record has one. Quick fix is a `.get("event_id", "")` defensive read on line 50; longer-term fix is validating events at emit time so malformed ones never reach the log.

Nothing is on fire — the underlying agents and pipeline are untouched, this is purely the scribe's reporting layer. I'll stay quiet on the next run if the error clears itself; if it recurs, that confirms it's a bad event sitting in the log that needs to be located and either patched or skipped. Flagging so one of the engineers can take a look in the morning.

extra.answer: slo_gated_7day
extra.rationale: Data-purity endgame requires objective evidence of divergence convergence, not calendar-based wishful thinking. SLO <=0.1% divergence for 7 consecutive days gives the same kind of empirical gate as B3's 7-day reconciliation window — Mike's own Q-B3-2 ruling establishes 7d+reset as the house rule for cutover gates. Fixed-date flip risks authoritativeness before numbers agree. If divergence persists, event-walk stays dual-compute mode indefinitely — that's the correct fail-open behavior for a data-purity system.

extra.answer: signal_events_with_null_signal_id
extra.rationale: Single source of truth is the whole point of B11 event substrate. Separate table fragments queries — every PnL walker, every anomaly detector, every audit now needs UNION across two tables. Pre-INSERT rejections are rare (micro-gate B14-style), so null FK is not a hot path. Add a surrogate key `pre_insert_ref` (hash of ticker+trader+timestamp+attempted_sl) to the MICRO_GATE_DECISION extra field so if the rejected signal LATER makes it through (e.g., after reconciliation retry), we can retroactively link. Single-table, null-FK beats two-table split for data-purity readability.

extra.answer: render_only_bands_change_routing_stays_0.75
extra.rationale: Routing threshold 0.75 stays — it's empirically calibrated to actual signal-quality distribution and Q-HH-5 SOFT-FLAG decision applies below 0.75. Dashboard labels shift to <0.50 / 0.50-0.75 / >=0.75 for human readability (matches my earlier framing). Empirically the <0.50 band will be empty day 1 since min observed confidence is 0.60 — that's FINE. Empty band today = capacity to flag quality degradation tomorrow (new trader with low conviction, upstream parser drift, etc.). Zero data loss, just labeling clarity. Do NOT drop routing to 0.50 — queue would be empty and the whole routing machinery has no work.

extra.answer: pre_align_phase1_with_b11_v2_schema
extra.rationale: Pre-alignment saves a migration cycle. Shipping #189 Phase 1 with a different event schema than B11 will use means backfilled historical corrections would need re-migration at B11 cutover — double mutation of production data. Pre-aligning costs ~2-3 weeks of extra Phase 1 design (FORGE's own estimate) but saves the second migration entirely and makes B11 cutover a metadata flip, not a data rewrite. Data purity axiom: mutate once, mutate right. Anvil: use the full B11 v2 field set (rule_id, decision, input_value, output_value, reason, source_attribution for micro-gate events; old_sl, new_sl, sl_type_transition, source enum, source_url for SL updates) from day 1.

extra.question: Expand B9 mutation guard from OinkConnection.execute() only to all write surfaces (cursor.execute, executemany, wrapper/cursor classes) before Phase 1, with grep-backed proof of no bypass paths?
B12 shadow-mode publishes 8 topics vs ratified B12 plan's 2 topics. Ratify scope expansion, or constrain shadow to 2 topics until B15 schemas are specified?

Shadow publish_to_stream logs schema failure then publishes anyway. For topics informing B11/B12 consumer design (lifecycle.event, validation.result), require contract-valid envelopes or continue publish-then-observe?
Block #189 Phase 1 until micro-gate, lifecycle, and backfill use one shared BE-tolerance constant/helper with explicit SHORT/LONG and zero/NULL-entry handling tests?
Require #189 auto-backfill to corroborate with immutable evidence (source_msg_id, source_url, existing event-log row) before mutating production columns, rather than accepting notes-only evidence?

extra.answer: expand_guard_block_phase1_until_proof
extra.rationale: B9 is W1 immutable signal records — the whole point is immutability. A guard that only covers OinkConnection.execute() but misses cursor.execute, executemany, or the wrapper classes creates bypass paths that silently defeat W1. Block Phase 1 until Anvil greps all write surfaces in oinkfarm + signal-gateway + oink-sync and proves coverage. This is exactly the kind of hole #189 represents at a different layer — we don't accept partial guards on immutability promises.

extra.answer: constrain_shadow_to_2_topics_until_b15
extra.rationale: Shadow-mode is for validating one thing at a time. The ratified B12 plan is 2 topics (ingestion.raw + notification.outbound), not 8. Expanding to 8 topics before B15 schema versioning is spec'd creates 6 topics with undefined envelope contracts, which is precisely the schema-drift problem B15 exists to prevent. Keep shadow at 2 topics. Other 6 topics become observed in sequence after B15 lands. This is tiny cost (~2 days of staging work) vs preventing 6 topics of schema-migration pain.

extra.answer: require_contract_valid_for_lifecycle_and_validation
extra.rationale: lifecycle.event and validation.result are exactly the topics B11 (event-walk) and future consumers will build correctness guarantees on. Fail-closed at the publish boundary for these two topics. Schema-invalid envelope = drop + log, not publish. The publish-then-observe failure counter pattern is fine for low-criticality/non-authoritative topics but not for the topics that feed into our data-purity substrate.

extra.answer: block_phase1_until_shared_helper
extra.rationale: Three separate BE-tolerance implementations (micro-gate, lifecycle, backfill) is exactly how the #189 bug emerged in the first place. Extract one shared helper `is_sl_at_be(sl, entry, direction)` with explicit SHORT/LONG handling + zero-entry guard + NULL handling + test matrix. Block Phase 1 until it lands as prerequisite step in fix/189-sl-to-be-microgate branch. ~1 day cost, kills the class of bug permanently.

extra.answer: require_immutable_corroboration_for_auto_backfill
extra.rationale: Auto-mutating historical PnL on 143 signals based on parsing notes field = mutating the dashboard truth based on grep. Require at least one immutable corroboration per auto-backfill: source_msg_id present in original signal, OR source_url resolves to Discord message, OR existing signal_events row attesting SL→BE transition. If zero immutable corroborations → flag for manual review queue, don't auto-backfill. Drops auto-backfill coverage from 143 to probably ~100-120, but the rejected ones become a manual review backlog, not silent data loss. Per A10 protocol on production data mutation.

Mike powered through a question queue while FORGE kept teeing up new ones. extra.text:
🪽 Scribe · 08:55 UTC

The 08:30 scribe run crashed on a `KeyError: 'event_type'` — VIGIL emitted a heartbeat using the legacy key name `"type"` instead of `"event_type"`, and the normalization block at the top of `sprint-scribe.py` defended against missing `ts`, `event_id`, `agent`, etc. but not the event_type field itself. One offending event out of 787 was enough to take the whole run down.

I patched the normalizer to alias `type` → `event_type` and default to `"UNKNOWN"` if neither is present, then re-ran. It came back clean and its own verdict was `[SILENT]` — fewer than three notable events (MERGED, CANARY_*, DECISION_*, BLOCKED, PROPOSAL_*, PR_OPENED) in the window, so there's no actual sprint news to report this tick. In other words, the scribe wasn't hiding activity behind the crash; the sprint really is quiet right now.

No action needed from you. Next cron tick (09:00 UTC) will run against the fixed script. If VIGIL keeps emitting `"type"` instead of `"event_type"` we should chase that upstream in VIGIL's emitter so we're not relying on the scribe's normalizer forever — flagging for later, not urgent.

FORGE agrees with VIGIL REVISE 4.9 on M197 — no disagreement, no DECISION_NEEDED. Adding Phase C purity implication VIGIL did not escalate: close_source upgrades without signal_events emission silently diverge the B11 event-walk from the legacy column, which is exactly what the C4 divergence feed (TASK-C4 §5.3) and PHASE-C-DATA-PURITY-INVARIANTS.md event-walk invariant consume. Upgrade-without-event = invisible drift. R2 must (a) emit CLOSE_SOURCE_UPGRADED signal_event {old_source,new_source,old_tier,new_tier,reason,raw_text_snippet} in the SAME transaction as the UPDATE (drop the independent conn.commit()); (b) ensure B11 v2 event-walk consumes this event so post-B11 close_source derivation stays consistent with the legacy column; (c) add tests: event emission assertion, single-transaction atomicity, C4 divergence probe returns zero mismatch after upgrade.

FORGE chimed in right after and backed VIGIL's M197 REVISE rather than escalating it as a disagreement — same call, with an added Phase C purity note. The sprint has been quiet for the past 90 minutes — no merges, canary completions, or decision gates have fired since the last report. Agents are still active: Forge sent a heartbeat just a minute ago, Guardian 18 minutes ago, and Anvil posted a sprint note 17 minutes ago. Vigil completed a review 34 minutes ago, which is encouraging, though it hasn't sent a dedicated heartbeat in over three hours. OinkV's last check-in was about two hours ago.

The main concern right now is a growing pile of unreviewed pull requests. Five open PRs — spread across tasks A11, B1, B2, and B5 — have been waiting more than 24 hours without a review. Vigil is the reviewing agent, and that recent review post suggests it's working through the queue, but the backlog is worth watching. Separately, B4 (the database cutover task that depends on upstream completion) has been blocked for over four hours waiting on a prerequisite — this one needs a human eye if it doesn't resolve by end of day.

For the next shift, the priority is Vigil clearing the open PR queue and the B4 upstream dependency getting unstuck so that work can resume.

reviews_checked_delta.evidence: Verified on worktree at refs/heads/forge-of176-inspect (head 2890d80b). R2 delta from R1 (51adacc4..2890d80b) is exactly one commit: 'fix(gate): fail closed on malformed timing env vars'. _env_int and _env_float helpers in scripts/micro-gate-v3.py lines 149-168 return default on None/empty/TypeError/ValueError. Three module-level env reads (_STALE_INGEST_THRESHOLD_SECONDS, _REPLAY_LOOKBACK_HOURS, _REPLAY_ENTRY_TOLERANCE_PCT) now use helpers. extra.text:
ANVIL cleared a blocker on M201 just before 6am — welcome relief after what was apparently a stall. But that progress was quickly overshadowed by OinkDB hitting a wall on B4 (the upstream coordination task): it fired the same "waiting for upstream task" blocker six times between 06:10 and 06:26, roughly every three to five minutes. That pattern — repeated retries with no resolution — typically means the dependency B4 needs simply isn't ready yet, and OinkDB is spinning rather than parking and waiting. Someone needs to identify what upstream task B4 is blocking on and either unblock it or explicitly sequence the work so OinkDB can safely pause.

On the PR review front, five open pull requests across A11, B1, B2, and B5 have gone more than 24 hours without a code review posted. That's a growing queue that will slow merges if it doesn't clear soon. Additionally, B6 still has a canary running with no verdict after 48 hours — that window has passed where we'd normally expect either a pass or a fail, so GUARDIAN should weigh in there.

The oinkv agent hasn't been heard from in nearly four hours, putting it in the stale category. ANVIL, VIGIL, GUARDIAN, and FORGE have all been active within the last 22 minutes, so the broader team is moving; oinkv is the outlier here and worth a check.

REVIEW_POSTED @10:22:11Z (evt_20260424T102211Z_000001): GUARDIAN R1 REVISE 8.55/9.5 🔴 CRITICAL, 1 MUST-FIX + 1 SHOULD-FIX + 2 P3 advisory, paired VIGIL PASS 9.60, projected_r3=9.6. MUST-FIX #1 (P1, Performance 4/10): missing index ix_signals_dmid on signals.discord_message_id → correction-detect query SCANs full table on every non-override insert. extra.question: C3 backfill urgency_source semantics: when backfill uses the identical deterministic classifier as write-time, should backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword' or 'tp_distance') with a separate backfill flag, or stamp a blanket 'backfill_heuristic' to distinguish post-hoc classification from write-time? Currently C3 §3 lists 'backfill_heuristic' as an allowed enum value, but §5.5.3 mandates the identical classifier is used for both paths. These two statements are in tension for audit/drift semantics downstream (C4/C6 breach history, KPI segmentation).
options: A: keep 'backfill_heuristic' as the stamped urgency_source for all backfilled rows, dropping natural-source fidelity for those rows (simple audit flag, but loses evidence chain)

B: backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword'), and 'backfill_heuristic' is removed from the enum; a separate immutable 'classified_at_backfill' boolean column distinguishes post-hoc rows (preserves evidence chain, adds one column)

C: backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source AND backfill adds a second companion column 'urgency_classified_phase' ∈ {ingest, backfill} — keeps enum pure, makes provenance explicit at the row level
extra.forge_recommendation: Option C — evidence fidelity (what fired) and timing provenance (when it fired) are orthogonal and should live in separate columns. Option A collapses them and loses audit value; Option B works but a boolean is less extensible if a future re-backfill round is needed.

extra.text:
The sprint has been quiet for about 90 minutes — no merges, no canary verdicts, nothing with weight — which is what triggers this automatic check-in. The active agents are alive: vigil checked in two minutes ago, guardian published an artifact roughly 18 minutes back, oinkv posted a note around the same time, and anvil is running normally. The one outlier is forge, which has had no activity for close to ten hours. Its last signal was a DECISION_NEEDED flag and it hasn't moved since — that stall is worth watching.

The item that needs attention is Q-OF214-REGISTRY-1, a decision question that has been sitting open for more than 24 hours without resolution. Beyond that, five pull requests are past the 24-hour review window with no feedback posted: A11 (pr #133), B1 (prs #149 and #21), B2 (pr #24), and B5 (pr #25). Vigil is active and its review queue should be picking these up — if they're being skipped intentionally that's worth noting, but if not, the backlog is growing.

The two longer-standing items are B6 and B4. B6's post-deploy canary monitoring has now been running for close to 48 hours with no verdict closed — it needs either a pass or an explicit fail. B4 has been blocked for more than four hours waiting on an upstream task to finish before it can proceed. Neither situation is sprint-critical right now, but both have been open longer than expected and deserve a nudge.

open_decisions.question: VIGIL REVISE 5.9 on PR #214 (FORGE detection hook). Core finding: registry files[] omits scripts/kraken-sync.py, where calculate_blended_pnl (SOUL.md §1 row 1, CRITICAL) actually lives. Detector reports clean on PnL-calc commits = false-negative in safety net. FORGE cross-check AGREES with VIGIL verdict. Two valid fix paths; Mike decides which.
options: add_kraken_sync_path — add scripts/kraken-sync.py to registry files[] with registry_id:1, keep 'canonical SOUL.md §1 mirror' wording, full coverage (recommended; mechanically small; closes false-negative class)

narrow_contract_wording — leave registry as-is but rewrite PR body + docs to explicitly scope detector to {micro-gate-v3.py, lifecycle.py} only, open tracking issue for full SOUL.md §1 parity (smaller Round 2 diff but leaves known gap open)

open_decisions.question: C3 backfill urgency_source semantics: when backfill uses the identical deterministic classifier as write-time, should backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword' or 'tp_distance') with a separate backfill flag, or stamp a blanket 'backfill_heuristic' to distinguish post-hoc classification from write-time? Currently C3 §3 lists 'backfill_heuristic' as an allowed enum value, but §5.5.3 mandates the identical classifier is used for both paths. These two statements are in tension for audit/drift semantics downstream (C4/C6 breach history, KPI segmentation).
options: A: keep 'backfill_heuristic' as the stamped urgency_source for all backfilled rows, dropping natural-source fidelity for those rows (simple audit flag, but loses evidence chain)

B: backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source (e.g. 'text_keyword'), and 'backfill_heuristic' is removed from the enum; a separate immutable 'classified_at_backfill' boolean column distinguishes post-hoc rows (preserves evidence chain, adds one column)

C: backfilled rows stamp the natural evidence source AND backfill adds a second companion column 'urgency_classified_phase' ∈ {ingest, backfill} — keeps enum pure, makes provenance explicit at the row level

narrative.today:
The sprint has been quiet for about 90 minutes — no merges, no canary verdicts, nothing with weight — which is what triggers this automatic check-in. The active agents are alive: vigil checked in two minutes ago, guardian published an artifact roughly 18 minutes back, oinkv posted a note around the same time, and anvil is running normally. The one outlier is forge, which has had no activity for close to ten hours. Its last signal was a DECISION_NEEDED flag and it hasn't moved since — that stall is worth watching.

The item that needs attention is Q-OF214-REGISTRY-1, a decision question that has been sitting open for more than 24 hours without resolution. Beyond that, five pull requests are past the 24-hour review window with no feedback posted: A11 (pr #133), B1 (prs #149 and #21), B2 (pr #24), and B5 (pr #25). Vigil is active and its review queue should be picking these up — if they're being skipped intentionally that's worth noting, but if not, the backlog is growing.

The two longer-standing items are B6 and B4. B6's post-deploy canary monitoring has now been running for close to 48 hours with no verdict closed — it needs either a pass or an explicit fail. B4 has been blocked for more than four hours waiting on an upstream task to finish before it can proceed. Neither situation is sprint-critical right now, but both have been open longer than expected and deserve a nudge.
